title: Basis Is Not Authority
excerpt: A parking sign states a condition. But a condition is not the same as the authority to enforce it. What a two-hour limit reveals about the gap between basis and legal power.
author: Ting Luo
body: The Missoula Public Library parking lot has a simple posted rule: two-hour parking. The Missoula Parking Commission patrols the lot and is authorized to ticket violators, and the city's parking fine materials treat "Library Lot — Posted 2hr" as an example of a sign-based violation.
So the basis exists. The sign is not imaginary. The rule is not imaginary. The parking limit is real.
But basis is not authority.

A basis answers one question: what rule, condition, or fact does the government point to? Authority answers a different question: who may turn that fact into a penalty, when, where, and under what legal grant of power?
Those questions are not the same.

What first made me notice the difference was the street parking just outside the library lot — separated only by a strip of landscaping and a sidewalk. The street signs are specific: they post the time-limited hours, and they make clear that before 8:00 a.m., after 5:00 p.m., and on weekends, no limit applies. The reason is visible once you see it: the Parking Commission's enforcement hours end at 5:00 p.m., and the street signs reflect that boundary.
The library lot sign does not. It says only "2 Hour Limit." No enforcement hours. No weekend exception. No indication of when the limit is actively enforced and when it is not.

That contrast raised a question I wanted to answer. So I ran a simple experiment. On a weekday, I parked in the library lot at 3:00 p.m. and stayed until around 8:00 p.m. If the two-hour limit were read by itself, the parking condition would be exceeded after 5:00 p.m. But the Parking Commission's published enforcement hours had already ended.
Nothing happened.

I tried again on a weekend. The two-hour sign did not disappear on Saturday or Sunday. The basis did not vanish. But the Parking Commission's own public materials describe enforcement as a weekday daytime function, excluding weekends and state holidays. I parked for the day.
Nothing happened.

That was not proof that the sign was meaningless. It was proof that the sign and the enforcement authority operate on different layers. The factual basis for a violation may exist while the enforcing body's authority to convert that basis into a citation does not. This is not a loophole. It is legality.
**The Undefined Boundary**

The same gap appears when a driver tries to obey a time limit by moving the vehicle. If the sign says "2 Hour Limit," the city may have a basis to say that a car cannot remain in the regulated space beyond two hours. But if the city wants to say that moving to another stall does not count, or that moving across the street does not count, or that the driver must leave the entire lot, block, district, or zone before the time limit resets, then the city must identify the rule that says so.
Same space? Same lot? Same block face? Same street? Same district? How long must the vehicle leave before returning?

If the government has defined those boundaries, it should show them. If the boundaries are not defined, then the driver is not violating a written rule by choosing one reasonable interpretation over another. The driver is encountering an unwritten expectation. And an unwritten expectation is not a basis for a penalty.
**Private Signs, Different Chains**
The same question follows you off public property.
One day, the parking lot at Clyde Coffee was full. Next door, a senior center lot had a sign: Senior Center Only. Violators Will Be Towed.
That sign created a different kind of basis. It told outsiders the space was not meant for them. It expressed a property boundary and a parking condition. But again — basis is not authority.

A sign saying "violators will be towed" does not itself explain the enforcement chain. Who may tow? Under what authority? Is this a private civil tow arranged by the property owner? Is it a law-enforcement abandoned-vehicle removal? Is there a city citation power? What procedure must be followed before the threat on the sign becomes an actual removal of the vehicle?

In Montana, the abandoned-vehicle statute draws a formal line. MCA § 61-12-401 allows certain law enforcement agencies to take a vehicle into custody if it has been abandoned for 48 hours or more on a public highway, or for 5 days or more on a city street, public property, or private property. Missoula's own abandoned-vehicle removal materials say the same thing in practical terms: if the vehicle is not owned by the property owner, it must have been on the property for at least five days before law enforcement may remove it.
So I parked there.
Nothing happened.

That result did not prove the sign was meaningless. It proved that a sign is not an immediately self-executing penalty. The sign may state a condition. The property owner may object. A legal pathway for removal may exist. But the question remains: what authority turns this parking fact into an enforceable consequence, and what procedure must be followed before that consequence occurs?
**When Government Interprets Itself**
This gets more complicated when a city tries to explain the law for the public.

The City of Billings has publicly stated that a private business owner may contact a towing company directly to remove a vehicle from private property, without waiting five days — and that this would be treated as a private civil matter rather than a law-enforcement removal. Billings has also said that if the tow company declines and the business owner then contacts Billings Police, the police will apply the five-day abandoned-vehicle sticker and follow the statutory procedure.
The subtext is clear: if you want to tow before five days, that is between you and the tow company, and whatever happens is your problem, not ours.

But this raises a familiar question. Billings is a city government. It is not a court. It is not a legislature. Its public statement is a policy position — an interpretation of how it believes the law works. That interpretation may be reasonable. It may even be correct. But it is not a judicial determination, and it does not bind anyone.

A city explaining what it thinks the law means is not the same as a court deciding what the law means. The public is left, once again, with a statement that looks like authority but functions as opinion.

This is the same structure at every level. A library parking sign states a condition but does not carry its own enforcement power after hours. A senior center sign threatens towing but does not identify the legal mechanism. A city government publishes an interpretation but does not have the power to make that interpretation final.
Basis. Interpretation. Opinion. None of them are authority until the law says they are.
**Why Small Rules Matter**

A two-hour parking limit is not a constitutional drama. It is not a criminal trial. It is not the kind of thing people usually associate with rule of law. But most people meet government through small rules first — a parking sign, a library policy, a city code, a citation under a windshield wiper, a payment portal, a sentence on a website saying enforcement happens during certain hours.
If those small rules are treated casually, then everyday law becomes casual too.

A parking system can have a sign, a policy, an enforcement body, and fines. But each part must do its own legal work. The sign gives notice of a condition. The ordinance or policy identifies the rule. The enforcement authority identifies who may act. The enforcement hours identify when that authority is publicly claimed. The citation must connect them. When those pieces are kept separate, ordinary people can understand the system. When they are blurred together, government power becomes fog.

In an earlier essay, I drew a distinction between formal legality and lived legality — between whether a rule exists somewhere and whether a rule is accessible enough to actually guide conduct. The same tension runs through a parking lot. A sign may satisfy formal legality. But if the enforcement boundaries, the reset conditions, and the temporal limits of authority are never made legible, then the system fails at the level of lived legality. The rule exists. The ability to understand and obey it does not.
The question is not whether a person should respect a two-hour limit. The question is whether government respects the difference between a limit and the authority to enforce it.

A sign may tell me what condition applies. A policy may tell me what the institution expects. A fact may show that a condition was exceeded. A city may publish what it believes the law means. But before any of them turns into a penalty, one question must be answered:
Who had authority to enforce this, here, now — and show me the law that says so.
That question is not defiance. It is the beginning of legality.
